title: Chapter Four: Blood
content:
> *"The Blade Sect and the Blood Flame Sect -- two of the Thirteen Demons, the dark mirror of the Nine Great Sects. Where the orthodox sects claim heaven's mandate, the Thirteen claim power earned through blood. They are not wrong. Nor are they forgivable."*
> -- Desolate Sect intelligence briefing, distributed to all field operatives

---

I don't go to the training ground.

Instead, I lock myself in my room, slide the wooden bolt into its bracket, and sit cross-legged on the bare floor. The planks are warm from the afternoon sun that streams through the single window -- a square of light that will track across the room as the hours pass, marking time I may not have.

I'm about to begin the most dangerous thing I've ever attempted.

Meditation. Real meditation -- not the body-strengthening cultivation I've been doing for years, but the kind where you open yourself to the energy of heaven and earth and try to make it yours. The kind where, if someone disturbs you at the wrong moment and your energy circulation tangles, you die.

*If someone disturbs me and my energy and blood flow get twisted, I'll die.*

From somewhere outside, the sound of my brothers' voices drifts through the window -- laughter, the thunk of a practice sword against a training post. Any of them could slam open my door without knocking. A servant could barge in to deliver a message. Any of them could kill me right now without even meaning to.

*That's a cheerful thought. Very motivating.*

I press my palms flat against my knees, straighten my spine, and sink inward.

The principles are clear in my mind -- I mapped them during breakfast, between bites of vegetables and the dizzying realization that I'd been building a vessel for years without knowing what to fill it with. Natural energy, inhaled through specialized breathing, refined through circulation, transformed into true qi that belongs to *me* and no one else.

The first breath goes in clean and comes out... different. I can feel the energy -- the raw, unfiltered qi of heaven and earth -- entering through my nose, passing through my lungs, and hitting the channels of my body like water hitting a system of pipes. The Dynamic Technique has made those channels wide and smooth, the internal walls hardened by years of circulation. The energy moves fast. Efficiently.

But it won't compress.

I try again. And again. Different breathing patterns, different intentions, different circulation paths. The energy enters, moves, and disperses. It won't condense. It won't layer. It won't become *denser* the way Yeo Il-shin's qi felt dense -- like the difference between fog and stone.

Frustration tightens my jaw. The headache, which had been lurking in the background all day, sharpens its teeth.

*Think. You're supposed to be a genius. Think.*

Hours pass. The square of sunlight slides across the floor, climbing my knee, crossing my folded hands, and eventually stretching up the far wall. Outside, the estate's daily rhythms play out in muffled sound -- a rooster at midday, the clatter of lunch being served, the rhythmic *thwack* of someone beating laundry against stone. My legs go numb and then stop being legs entirely, becoming abstract concepts that I'm dimly aware of below the waist. The energy continues to cycle through my body -- entering, moving, dispersing, refusing to become what I need it to become.

And then, somewhere around the time the light turns amber and the shadows swallow the room, I stop trying to force it and just... listen.

To my own body. To the channels I built. To the energy flowing through them, and the way it behaves when I stop pushing.

*Oh.*

It was already condensing. Not in the dantian, where I'd been trying to force it -- but in the Baihui point. The gate at the crown of my skull. The energy was pooling there naturally, drawn by the wide-open aperture, concentrating and intensifying in exactly the way I wanted it to, just in exactly the wrong place.

My body has been doing what I asked. My *condition* has been stealing the results.

The realization is a bucket of ice water dumped down my spine.

"...Huh? This is strange."

My voice sounds far away when I finally speak. I unfold my legs -- they feel light, as if I'd just sat down, though I've been here since morning. The cross-legged position should have left me stiff and sore. It didn't.

But something else is wrong.

*Why is the Baihui acupoint so wide open?*

I probe inward, examining the energy pathways through my skull -- the crown point, the upper Dantian, the channels that connect my brain to the rest of my body's meridian system. What I find makes my stomach drop through the floor.

The gate at the top of my head -- the one the Elf doctor described fifteen years ago as "completely open" -- has gotten *worse.* Massively worse. The energy pouring through my Baihui point has increased to a torrent, and the upper Dantian is struggling to contain it. I've been so focused on the Dynamic Technique, so obsessed with strengthening my body and creating new martial arts, that I haven't checked this in over a year.

*I knew mine was growing abnormally.*

The math is brutal. Comparing the rate of expansion to the capacity of my upper Dantian -- the brain's energy center, the thing that keeps me *conscious* and *alive* and *me* --

Less than five years.

Less than five years before the energy overwhelms my upper Dantian entirely. Before I lose consciousness and never get it back. Before the flood of heaven's energy drowns the part of me that thinks and feels and creates sword techniques in the dark.

Less than five years. I'm fifteen.

Twenty. The Elf was right.

"Am I going to die like this?"

The words come out thin and strange. The room has gone dark around me -- the sun set while I was meditating, and I never lit a lamp. My hands are shaking as I press them against the floorboards and push myself to standing. My knees pop. The blood rushes back into my legs in a searing wave of pins and needles, and I stagger toward the door.

*Die? Me?*

The door handle is cold under my fingers. I turn it.

Click.

And the smell hits me.

Blood. Thick, metallic, wet -- the smell of a butchered animal, but wrong somehow. Too much of it. Too widespread. The scent rolls in with the night air like fog, coating my throat, making my stomach clench.

I grab my sword. I don't think about it -- my hand knows where the blade is before my mind gives the order.

No corpses in sight. Not yet. I pass the training ground -- the wooden pillar rack stands like a skeleton in the moonlight -- and move carefully through the estate's darkened corridors. The lanterns along the covered walkway have burned out, all of them. Nobody refilled the oil. The silence presses against my ears like hands trying to muffle a scream. The estate is never this quiet. Even at night, there are guards, servants, the distant sound of someone snoring.

Nothing. Just the creak of roof tiles cooling in the night air and the distant, indifferent chirp of crickets in the soybean fields.

The silence has a weight to it. A *texture.* Like walking into a room where something terrible has already happened and the air hasn't figured out how to stop holding its breath.

My feet are moving before I tell them to. The Dynamic Technique floods my muscles with energy -- not because I'm activating it, but because my body has learned to treat silence as a threat. Every hair on my arms is standing. The skin on the back of my neck prickles with something primal and animal, something that knows about danger before the rational mind has finished forming the question.

*Please let me be wrong. Please let this be paranoia. Please let there be a reasonable explanation for why the estate smells like a slaughterhouse and sounds like a grave.*

I'm not wrong.

I round the corner into the main courtyard, and the world tilts.

Corpses.

The saying in the martial world is that blood forms a river. That's an exaggeration -- a dramatic one, the kind of thing storytellers say to make their audiences gasp. The reality is uglier and more mundane: blood soaks into dirt, pools in the cracks between flagstones, drips from the thresholds of annex buildings in thin, dark lines.

Family members lie scattered across the courtyard like discarded dolls. Some are face-down. Some are face-up, eyes open, mouths frozen in expressions I don't want to catalog. Each one has the same wound -- a sword strike across the chest, identical and precise, as if someone used them for practice.

Boom.

A thunderclap of impact from outside the main gate, followed by the unmistakable ring of sword meeting sword. Energy -- massive, devastating energy -- crackles through the air, and I know from the scale of it that whoever's fighting out there is operating at a level the Jeong family couldn't touch on its best day.

My stomach lurches. Bile rises in my throat and I swallow it down hard, tasting acid and iron. My hands are shaking -- the same hands that broke a wrist and created a swift sword technique and held a practice blade with absolute steadiness are *shaking* -- and I grip the sword hilt until the trembling stops.

I know these people. That crumpled form near the well is Old Zhang, the gatekeeper, who was the only servant willing to bring my meals without being ordered to. The two bodies tangled near the annex door are the wives of guest disciples -- women who had nothing to do with martial arts, who were just *here* because this was home.

Something inside me cracks. Not breaks -- *cracks.* A fissure line running through the wall I've spent fifteen years building, the wall that separates the part of me that feels from the part of me that survives.

And then the wall seals itself shut. Fast. Automatic. Like a reflex I didn't know I had.

*You can break down later. Right now, you survive.*

I tighten my grip on the iron sword and keep walking. My sandals stick to the flagstones where the blood has gone tacky. Through the courtyard, past Old Zhang's body -- I step around him, not over him -- past the annex door and the tangled limbs there. The night air should be cool but it's not; the metallic heat of all this spilled blood has warmed it, thick and sweet and wrong.

*Five years,* the voice in my head adds, unbidden and vicious. *You were going to die anyway. What's a few less corpses between friends?*

The gallows humor tastes like ash. I use it anyway. It's all I have.

Around ten men sit in a loose cluster near the main building. Bandits, by the look of them -- dark clothes, weapons visible, the careless posture of people who've already won and are waiting for the cleanup. One of them wears twin swords crossed on his back. He turns his head when I come within ten paces.

"Who are you?"

"The third son of this household."

No answer. The twin-sword man rises and approaches, his stride heavy with the casual confidence of a predator who hasn't been challenged in a long time. He doesn't bother drawing his weapons. He just raises one hand, charges it with internal energy, and swings down at my skull.

My eyes flare sky-blue.

I can *see* it -- the flow of his qi, the trajectory of his strike, the way the energy pools in his palm before release. Not metaphorically. Literally. The world slows down and the man's attack draws itself in lines of light across my vision.

I catch his wrist. Strike down with the back of my hand. Twist.

Grappling. I've never formally learned it. But the movements flow out of me like water finding its path downhill -- grab, redirect, lock. The Dynamic Technique floods my body with energy, hardening my grip until it's a vice of bone and compressed muscle.

*Kwak.*

The sound of his wrist breaking is louder than I expected. He screams. Every head in the courtyard snaps toward us.

"Let go!"

"You bastard!"

They're already charging. The twin-sword man, howling in pain, draws a blade with his remaining good hand and swings at my head -- and something inside me *ignites.*

Not just the Dynamic Technique. Something beyond it.

The intention to move my qi solidifies into something I can almost touch -- a framework, a structure, an *identity* that names itself in my mind with the clarity of a bell struck in silence:

*Scripture of Overcoming the Origin.*

The name arrives without thought, without reasoning. It simply *is* -- a true energy circulation method that breaks through the current limits, that takes everything the Dynamic Technique built and pushes it past the wall I've been pressing against for months.

Energy floods from my right foot and left hand to my right hand and left foot, overlapping, doubling in density. My left toes shatter the flagstones beneath me and I *move* -- faster than I've ever moved, faster than the moonlight can follow -- and the iron sword in my hand traces a line of silver through the dark.

Three heads leave three bodies. The twin-sword man, the two men flanking him -- all caught in a single sweep of the swift sword technique.

*Oh.*

The heads are still in the air. Three pairs of eyes, still wide with shock, still tracking the blade that killed them. I watch them fall and feel -- nothing. Not horror, not satisfaction. Just the cool, clinical awareness that my body has done something extraordinary and my mind hasn't caught up yet.

*It felt like it wasn't my own body.*

A red-haired woman lunges from behind the falling corpses, a crescent-shaped wheel in her left hand. I pivot, twist, and drive the iron sword through her throat before she can complete her swing.

My breathing hasn't changed. Not a single extra beat. The Dynamic Technique keeps my body stable and grounded, my heartrate even, my muscles oxygenated and responsive. It's the most terrifying thing about this body -- it doesn't register what I've just done as anything worth reacting to. Four people dead by my hand, and my pulse hasn't flickered.

*You just killed four people.*

The thought goes in the drawer.

*Their heads are on the ground.*

I flick the blood off the iron blade. It spatters against the flagstones in a line of dark droplets. Drawer.

*You're fifteen years old and you just decapitated three men in a single stroke.*

Drawer. Lock. Key. Gone.

The shock will come later. But that moment is not now. Now there are six more enemies in this courtyard, their eyes white in the torchlight, and I am the only one standing between whatever comes next and the few survivors who might still be breathing somewhere in this estate.

"You..."

A thin-bodied swordsman watching from the side distorts his face in something between fury and disbelief. But it's the large man behind him who rises -- a giant with a beast's physique and fox-sharp eyes, one hand on the thin man's shoulder in a gesture that says *I'll handle this.*

"You've devoted your life to the swift sword," the giant says, studying me. "You have no other tricks. You're just a young and well-trained swift swordsman. Those guys -- they were caught off guard."

Not everything he says is true. But he's not entirely wrong, either -- the Scripture of Overcoming the Origin is seconds old, and the swift sword is my only offensive technique. If this man is what I think he is, I'm out of tricks.

"Still, it's amazing. Truly amazing. How can he do that at his age?"

"Blood Flame Sect?" I ask, glancing at the red-haired woman at my feet.

"No. I am from the Blade Sect."

My blood goes cold. The Blade Sect -- one of the Thirteen Demons, the great unorthodox powers that rival the Nine Sects themselves.

"You are from the Blade Sect? This is definitely the Blood Flame Sect."

"That's right."

"What are two of the Thirteen Demons doing in a place like Xinye County?"

"It's none of your business."

"Why are you answering every question?"

"Because it's a waste." The man laughs -- a rough, honest sound that doesn't match the blood on the ground around us. "A martial artist as well-trained as you at your age is rare. It's also amusing to see a kid who grew up in the countryside talking as if he's experienced the center of the martial world."

"A waste?"

I let a sneer curl my lips. My heart is beating so hard I can feel it in my teeth, but my face -- thank every god in every heaven -- my face stays cold.

"You're probably just afraid of my maternal family." I shift my weight forward onto my front foot -- a swordsman's stance, aggressive, deliberate. "You bastard of the wicked wanderers without roots."

This is the bet.

Everything I have on a single card: the fact that my maternal grandfather was the retired Sect Commander of the Desolate Sect, an organization so powerful that even the Thirteen Demons calculate around it. The Blade Sect man didn't kill me on sight. The twin-sword man used a hand strike instead of drawing steel. *They were ordered not to kill the third son.*

If I'm right, I live. If I'm wrong -- well. Less than five years was always going to be a short ride.

"...You crazy little brat."

The growl that comes from the giant's chest is barely human. The energy rolling off him makes my skin prickle and my lizard brain scream *run,* but I lock my knees and keep my expression carved from stone.

"You bastards can't kill me. No matter what I do, it's the same."

*BANG.*

The main gate explodes inward. Shattered door panels cartwheel through the air, and a body rolls with the debris -- one of the Zhongnan Sect disciples, the one called Precipice Sword. His uniform is torn, blood runs from the corner of his mouth, and his eyes have the glazed intensity of a man who's been fighting something beyond his weight class.

*Even a master like him is in that state?*

Then the fence erupts.

Yeo Il-shin and the other Zhongnan disciple come crashing through the wall in a shower of splintered wood. They're bleeding. Badly. The sight of Yeo Il-shin -- immortal, ageless, the woman I thought was a sword fairy descended from heaven -- with the top of her left ear *sliced off* is the most shocking thing I've seen tonight, and I'm standing in a field of corpses.

And behind them -- walking through the hole in the fence with the unhurried stride of someone who owns the world and knows it -- comes the source.

A man. Clearly human. Holding a massive sword from which an aura of pure death ripples outward like heat shimmer. The grass beneath his feet has flattened, pressed down by the sheer weight of his energy, and the torches on either side of the ruined fence gutter and go out.

The energy is beyond anything I can comprehend. Not just strong -- *absolute.* The kind of presence that makes the air itself bow, that tells every living thing in range that the center of the universe is *here,* in this body, in this blade.

My knees buckle. I lock them. My vision swims -- the courtyard tilting sideways -- and the Baihui point at the crown of my skull *screams.* A sudden, vicious spike of pressure, as if the energy pouring through the gate has recognized something in this man's presence and is responding to it, amplifying, resonating like a struck bell.

For half a second, the world goes white. Pure, blinding, sky-blue white.

I blink. The world comes back, dark and sharp-edged. The Blade Sect Leader is still standing there, still radiating death, still holding the sword that wounded an ageless Elf.

*My head is going to kill me before he does.*

"Sect Leader!"

The Blade Sect giant drops to his knees. And the world clicks into place.

*The leader of the Blade Sect. In person.*

One of the Thirteen Demons. The kind of power that makes elders of the Nine Sects bleed.

Everyone stops moving. The compound goes still, suspended between one heartbeat and the next, caught in the gravitational field of something too massive to fight.

Everyone stops.

Except me.

I move. My legs are running before my brain finishes the calculation -- toward the Blade Sect giant, the one still kneeling, the one who was afraid to kill me. The Scripture of Overcoming the Origin blazes through my meridians and I launch myself forward with everything the Dynamic Technique has ever given me.

"Wha-- what?"

The giant's eyes go wide. He's scrambling to rise. Too slow.

The iron sword punches through his back and out through his abdomen.

He collapses. I plant my foot on his spine and look up -- directly into the Blade Sect Leader's abyss-dark eyes.

*He didn't stop me. He watched me do it.*

The jade-blue lightning in my eyes crackles once, then fades.

"Even now," I say.

My voice is steady. My heart is trying to tear itself free of my ribcage.

"Can you kill me?"
